title: Does CLIP’s generalization performance mainly stem from high train-test similarity?

reviews.summary:
This paper tries to uncover the reason for the good generalization of foundation models like CLIP. Specifically, we see that CLIP has a good zero-shot accuracy on OOD datasets, which is much higher compared to the models that are first trained on the labeled dataset (ID), such as ImageNet, and then tested on OOD datasets, such as ImageNet-Sketch.

As the pre-training data is large, it is highly likely to contain similar images to the OOD dataset. If that is the case, we might not call the test OOD dataset to be truly out-of-distribution, which might be the reason for the good performance of CLIP. 

Authors try to find an answer to the above problem through various experiments. 

They define the perceptual similarity of two images using the CLIP ViT-B/16 embedding of a pre-trained CLIP model. They find that the general OOD datasets like ImageNet Sketch have more common images with the LAION dataset than the ImageNet dataset.

In the first experiment, the authors removed images similar to the OOD dataset from the LAION dataset iteratively and measured the performance of models trained on a pruned dataset. As expected, they found a general trend of performance decrease, indicating that similar images in the training dataset had more importance.

The second experiment compared the models trained on small datasets such as ImageNet vs those trained on LAION. The authors define a metric called generalization gap, which is the set of minimum distance of each image in the test set with every image in the training set. They then remove the data points from the larger dataset (here, LAION) to have the same generalization gap as that of the ImageNet with the OOD dataset. They then trained a CLIP model on the pruned dataset. 
They discovered some performance drops, but the performance was still high compared to the case where the model was just trained on the ImageNet training set. This led to them concluding that the high performance of the CLIP is mostly due to more training data rather than test train similarity.
reviews.strengths: The paper is very well written. The experiments are clearly defined, and the motivation for each experiment is mentioned.
reviews.weaknesses:
Even though authors perform many experiments, the experiments are performed on a single type of dataset, which are the variations of the ImageNet.
It would have been to include experiments with other OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. Without these experiments it is not sure if these analysis is limited to one type of dataset.
reviews.questions:
1. Are there experiments on other datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Having these experiments would be better to make a strong case.
2. Why do we use only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to filter? Won't larger CLIP models have a better embedding to filter out the data points?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

Summary:
This paper tries to uncover the reason for the good generalization of foundation models like CLIP. Specifically, we see that CLIP has a good zero-shot accuracy on OOD datasets, which is much higher compared to the models that are first trained on the labeled dataset (ID), such as ImageNet, and then tested on OOD datasets, such as ImageNet-Sketch.

As the pre-training data is large, it is highly likely to contain similar images to the OOD dataset. If that is the case, we might not call the test OOD dataset to be truly out-of-distribution, which might be the reason for the good performance of CLIP. 

Authors try to find an answer to the above problem through various experiments. 

They define the perceptual similarity of two images using the CLIP ViT-B/16 embedding of a pre-trained CLIP model. They find that the general OOD datasets like ImageNet Sketch have more common images with the LAION dataset than the ImageNet dataset.

In the first experiment, the authors removed images similar to the OOD dataset from the LAION dataset iteratively and measured the performance of models trained on a pruned dataset. As expected, they found a general trend of performance decrease, indicating that similar images in the training dataset had more importance.

The second experiment compared the models trained on small datasets such as ImageNet vs those trained on LAION. The authors define a metric called generalization gap, which is the set of minimum distance of each image in the test set with every image in the training set. They then remove the data points from the larger dataset (here, LAION) to have the same generalization gap as that of the ImageNet with the OOD dataset. They then trained a CLIP model on the pruned dataset. 
They discovered some performance drops, but the performance was still high compared to the case where the model was just trained on the ImageNet training set. This led to them concluding that the high performance of the CLIP is mostly due to more training data rather than test train similarity.

Strengths:
The paper is very well written. The experiments are clearly defined, and the motivation for each experiment is mentioned.

Weaknesses:
Even though authors perform many experiments, the experiments are performed on a single type of dataset, which are the variations of the ImageNet.
It would have been to include experiments with other OOD datasets such as iWILDCam or FMoW. Without these experiments it is not sure if these analysis is limited to one type of dataset.

Questions:
1. Are there experiments on other datasets unrelated to ImageNet? Having these experiments would be better to make a strong case.
2. Why do we use only CLIP ViT-B/16 embeddings to filter? Won't larger CLIP models have a better embedding to filter out the data points?

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper investigates whether CLIP's exceptional zero-shot generalization performance on out-of-distribution (OOD) benchmarks stems primarily from high train-test similarity. The authors hypothesize that web-scale datasets like LAION-400M contain many images highly similar to common OOD benchmarks (e.g., ImageNet-Sketch, ImageNet-R), potentially explaining CLIP's strong performance. To test this hypothesis, they introduce a "similarity gap" framework that enables systematic removal of highly similar images from LAION while matching ImageNet-Train's similarity distribution to test sets. Surprisingly, CLIP trained on these pruned datasets maintains substantial performance (e.g., 44.78% on ImageNet-Sketch vs. expected ~14% based on ImageNet-trained models), suggesting that high train-test similarity alone cannot fully explain CLIP's generalization. The work also identifies a 100M subset of LAION that maintains near-full performance, offering a practical contribution for future research.

## Strengths

The paper addresses an important and timely question about foundation model generalization, challenging a fundamental assumption about CLIP's OOD robustness. The research question is well-motivated and clearly articulated, with compelling initial evidence (Figure 1) showing that LAION contains more similar nearest neighbors to OOD datasets than ImageNet-Train does.

The methodology is rigorous and well-designed. The similarity gap framework (Section 4.4) is elegantly formalized and provides a principled approach to isolate the effect of highly similar images while controlling for dataset size differences—a key confound that could otherwise obscure the analysis. The framework is mathematically sound and the experimental design thoughtfully addresses potential alternative explanations through multiple pruning strategies (near/far/random pruning).

The experimental validation is comprehensive, with extensive ablations across multiple ImageNet-based OOD benchmarks and evaluation on non-ImageNet datasets (MNIST, SVHN, CelebA, Waterbirds). The correlation analysis in Figure 3 convincingly demonstrates that nearest-neighbor similarity predicts CLIP's performance, providing strong initial support for the hypothesis. The work is reproducible with promised code release and thorough experimental descriptions, including detailed hyperparameters and clear methodology sections.

The paper is exceptionally well-written with clear motivation for each experiment and excellent visualizations that build intuition. The practical contribution of the 100M LAION core set could be valuable for future research and reduces computational requirements for practitioners. The authors also demonstrate commendable honesty in discussing limitations and open questions, avoiding overclaiming.

## Weaknesses

Despite the paper's strengths, several important limitations constrain its impact and generalizability.

**Limited Scope and Domain Specificity**: The analysis focuses almost exclusively on ImageNet-based OOD benchmarks and zero-shot classification. While Appendix D briefly explores CelebA and Waterbirds, these analyses are secondary to the main narrative. The authors justify focusing on ImageNet in Appendix C, but this choice limits confidence in the generalizability of findings. Whether conclusions hold for truly different domains (medical imaging, satellite imagery, remote sensing) or for other tasks (retrieval, visual question answering, object detection) remains unclear. The paper would be significantly strengthened by including experiments on datasets like iWILDCam or FMoW that represent fundamentally different visual domains, not merely stylistic variations of natural images.

**Similarity Metric Dependency and Potential Circularity**: While Appendix E provides ablations comparing CLIP ViT-B/16+ and ViT-L-14 embeddings, the entire analysis depends critically on using CLIP's own embedding space as the similarity metric. This creates a potential circularity: using CLIP embeddings to evaluate what makes CLIP generalize well may bias results in ways that are difficult to detect. The authors acknowledge they cannot guarantee all highly similar images were removed. Alternative similarity metrics—such as those from other vision models (DINO, MAE), human perceptual judgments, or psychophysical measures—are not thoroughly explored. The choice of ε=0.05 for near-duplicate detection also appears somewhat arbitrary, and sensitivity to this threshold deserves more analysis.

**Incomplete Mechanistic Understanding**: While the paper convincingly demonstrates that high train-test similarity is insufficient to explain CLIP's performance, it provides limited insight into what actually drives generalization. The discussion mentions scale, diversity, and density as likely factors but leaves these largely unexplored. The compositionality question—whether CLIP generalizes from sketches of cats and natural images of dogs to sketches of dogs—is acknowledged as important but not investigated. This leaves a significant explanatory gap: the paper tells us what CLIP's generalization is NOT caused by, but not what it IS caused by. A deeper investigation into these alternative mechanisms would substantially strengthen the contribution.

**Confounding Factors in Pruning**: When removing highly similar images, the paper simultaneously removes a specific subset of training data with particular distributional properties. The pruned datasets are not just lower in similarity but also potentially different in diversity, quality, caption characteristics, and representation of different visual domains. It remains unclear whether performance drops are due to similarity removal or loss of beneficial properties of that subset. The observation that far-pruning (removing dissimilar images) sometimes slightly improves performance suggests that dissimilar images may act as noise, but this is not systematically characterized.

**Modest Performance Drops and Ambiguous Interpretation**: For ImageNet-Sketch (the most stylistically different benchmark), removing highly similar images causes a 10.8% relative performance drop (from 50.21% to 44.78%). While framed as "surprisingly high," this is non-trivial and suggests similarity plays a meaningful role. Additionally, the "combined-pruned" experiment (which removes highly similar images for all six test sets simultaneously) shows larger drops (10.8% for Sketch, 4.8% for R) than individual pruning, suggesting interactions not fully explored. The paper's framing that "high similarity cannot fully explain performance" is correct but somewhat conservative. A more nuanced discussion of where the boundary lies between "insufficient" and "non-negligible" would clarify the practical importance of similarity.

**Limited Analysis of Model Scale and Architecture**: Only ViT-B/32 models are trained despite being smaller than commonly deployed CLIP variants (ViT-L/14, ViT-g/14). The paper uses CLIP ViT-B/16+ embeddings for computing similarity but trains ViT-B/32 models, introducing a mismatch whose impact is not thoroughly characterized. How findings generalize to larger models and other vision-language models (ALIGN, BLIP) remains unclear. This limits the applicability to modern foundation model deployments.

**Statistical Rigor and Practical Limitations**: No confidence intervals or statistical significance tests are provided for the reported accuracies. Performance differences on some benchmarks are modest (e.g., <1% for several datasets in Table 1), and it is unclear whether these are statistically significant. Furthermore, the pruning methodology requires access to test sets a priori, which is impractical for truly unknown distributions. While the authors acknowledge this limitation, it significantly constrains the real-world applicability of the framework.

## Questions and Suggestions

1. How sensitive are the main conclusions to the choice of CLIP ViT-B/16+ embeddings for computing similarity? Have you considered alternative metrics (DINO, MAE, human perceptual judgments) that might yield different pruning decisions?

2. Why does the "combined-pruned" setup show substantially larger performance drops than individual test-set pruning? Does this suggest the pruning method struggles to balance multiple test sets, or that there are synergistic effects between different types of similar images?

3. Can you provide statistical significance tests (e.g., bootstrap confidence intervals) for the performance differences in Table 1, particularly for modest drops?

4. The paper argues that scale and diversity drive generalization but provides limited direct evidence. Have you considered experiments that directly manipulate diversity or measure dataset diversity metrics?

5. For the compositionality question: could you design an experiment testing whether CLIP generalizes from sketches of similar objects to sketches of novel objects? This would clarify whether compositional generalization explains the remaining 30-40% performance gap.

6. How do results change with different CLIP architectures (ViT-L, ViT-H) or other vision-language models? Is the finding specific to CLIP or more general?

7. Can you expand the analysis beyond ImageNet-based benchmarks? Experiments on truly different domains (medical imaging, satellite imagery) would substantially strengthen the claims.

8. The near-duplicate threshold (ε=0.05) appears arbitrary. How sensitive are conclusions to this choice? Is there a sharp transition or gradual degradation in performance?

9. How would practitioners apply this framework to novel OOD benchmarks without access to test sets? Is there a way to estimate which samples to prune without retraining?

## Minor Issues

The paper could benefit from more rigorous quantification of the unexplained performance gap and clearer discussion of what fraction of CLIP's advantage is explained by factors other

reviews.summary: This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.
reviews.questions: What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the generalization behaviour of CLIP from the perspective of the training set. CLIP, as a pioneering foundation model, is well known for its exceptional generalization capability. However, it remains unclear whether such a good generalization capability stems from the web-scale training set, as it may well enclose samples similar to those on the test sets. This paper tackles this question by filtering out similar samples according to CLIP-embedding-based nearest neighbors. By creating a LAION subset that comes with as large a generalization gap as the ImageNet-1K training set, this work concludes that it is other factors, rather than the model has already seen samples with similar distribution, that leads to the outstanding ood generalization of CLIP models.

Strengths:
1. This paper first studies the ood generalization behaviour of CLIP from the interesting perspective of train-test distribution similarity, and has drawn some intriguing conclusions, like a subset of 100M of LAION-400M is able to train a good-performing model on ood benchmarks.
2. The experiments are well motivated and designed, resulting in compelling results that even without nearest neighbor samples, CLIP is still to maintain good performance on ood benchmarks.

Weaknesses:
1. This paper only works on common classification ood benchmarks, but neglecting a whole bunch of other CLIP application domains. In the very least, retrieval is the most foundamental test ground to probe how good a CLIP model is. The authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for sample filtering, and use Flickr-30k as the out-of-domain dataset. Also, experiments on more diverse benchmarks, like VTAB (check datacomp paper for current best practices), are encouraged.
2. Some crucial experimental details seem to be missing. For instance, how the nearest neighbor sets in section 4.1 are constructed are not mentioned at all (or mentioned in the appendix). How many samples are chosen in this set, and what is the threshold of CLIP score used here?
3. The authors should compare the similarity of nearest neighbors to test sets between the whole LAION datset (after filtering in section 5/6) and ImageNet-Train. Otherwise, one could argue that, even though the closest sample in LAION and ImageNet training set is about the same far away to the test set, LAION has a lot more samples close to the closest sample than ImageNet (but not as close to the test set as the closest sample), and thus still has a unfair advantage on those ood benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion.

Questions:
What is the potential application of the finding in this paper? I know this is a pure analytical work, and believe its merit is above the aceeptance threshold of ICLR. But I am still intrigued to learn what is the broader impact of this work, like how this would enlighten future research or engineer endeavours.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper investigates whether CLIP's exceptional out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization performance stems primarily from high train-test similarity. The authors hypothesize that web-scale datasets like LAION-400M contain many samples similar to common OOD benchmarks (ImageNet-Sketch, ImageNet-R, etc.), potentially inflating apparent generalization capabilities. To test this hypothesis rigorously, they introduce a 'similarity gap' framework that enables surgical removal of highly similar images from LAION-400M while controlling for dataset size by matching ImageNet-Train's similarity distribution to test sets. Surprisingly, CLIP trained on these pruned datasets maintains substantial performance (e.g., 44.78% on ImageNet-Sketch vs. 49.39% baseline, compared to ~14% expected from ImageNet-trained models), suggesting that high train-test similarity alone cannot fully explain CLIP's generalization. The work also identifies a 100M subset of LAION that preserves near-full performance, providing a practical contribution to the community.

## Strengths

1. **Novel and important research question**: The paper addresses a fundamental assumption about CLIP's generalization by rigorously examining whether apparent OOD robustness is primarily inflated by incidental train-test similarity. This is a valuable and timely contribution to understanding foundation models.

2. **Rigorous and well-motivated experimental design**: The similarity gap framework (Section 4.4) is elegant and principled, providing a formal mechanism to isolate the effect of highly similar images while maintaining fair comparisons across datasets of different sizes. The methodology is mathematically sound and the experimental design includes multiple pruning strategies (near/far/random) that provide complementary evidence.

3. **Comprehensive experimental validation**: The work includes extensive experiments across six OOD benchmarks with thorough ablations examining different embedding choices (Appendix E compares ViT-B/16+ and ViT-L-14), analyses on additional datasets (CelebA, Waterbirds in Appendix D), and diverse pruning strategies. The correlation analysis in Figure 3 convincingly demonstrates that nearest-neighbor similarity predicts CLIP's performance. The striking 30 percentage point gap between expected (14%) and observed (44.78%) accuracy on ImageNet-Sketch provides compelling empirical evidence for the main conclusion.

4. **Reproducibility and practical contribution**: Code and datasets are promised to be released with detailed experimental descriptions. The 100M LAION core set is a useful practical contribution that could facilitate future research while reducing computational requirements. The work demonstrates honest discussion of limitations and open questions rather than overclaiming.

5. **Clear presentation**: The paper is well-written with effective visualizations (Figures 1-5) that build intuition progressively. The authors acknowledge key limitations explicitly rather than glossing over them.

## Weaknesses

1. **Limited scope of evaluation**: The analysis focuses almost exclusively on ImageNet-based OOD benchmarks and zero-shot classification. While the authors justify this choice in Appendix C by arguing that ImageNet distribution shifts are "more human aligned," this justification is somewhat subjective and deserves quantitative support (e.g., inter-human agreement on whether test sets constitute meaningful distribution shifts). Critically, the paper neglects other foundational CLIP application domains. Retrieval tasks are particularly important—the authors could use MSCOCO as the in-domain dataset for filtering and Flickr-30k as the OOD evaluation set. Experiments on more diverse benchmarks like VTAB (as noted in the DataComp paper's best practices) would substantially strengthen the claims. The brief CelebA and Waterbirds analysis in Appendix D is promising but too limited to draw firm conclusions about generalization to truly different domains (medical imaging, satellite imagery, remote sensing, etc.).

2. **Incomplete mechanistic understanding**: While the paper convincingly demonstrates that high train-test similarity is insufficient to explain CLIP's performance, it provides limited insight into what actually drives generalization. The discussion mentions scale, diversity, and density as likely factors but these remain largely unexplored. The compositionality question—whether CLIP generalizes from sketches of cats and natural images of dogs to sketches of dogs—is acknowledged as important but left entirely open. The paper successfully refutes one hypothesis but does not identify what replaces it, leaving a significant gap in understanding. This is a fundamental limitation: the paper tells us what CLIP's generalization is NOT caused by, but not what it IS caused by.

3. **Similarity metric dependency and potential circularity**: While Appendix E provides ablations comparing different embedding spaces, the entire analysis critically depends on CLIP ViT-B/16+ embeddings as the similarity metric. Using CLIP embeddings to evaluate CLIP's training data creates a potential circularity—the metric is derived from the model being studied. The authors cannot guarantee that all highly similar images were removed, and the choice of ε=0.05 for near-duplicate detection appears somewhat arbitrary. Beyond the provided ablations, have the authors considered perceptual metrics from human studies or alternative embedding spaces from other vision models (DINO, MAE)? The correlation between embedding similarity and actual visual similarity in Figure 2, while visually convincing, lacks quantitative validation through human perceptual studies.

4. **Missing crucial experimental details**: Several important methodological details are either absent or relegated to appendices. Specifically:
   - The construction of nearest neighbor sets in Section 4.1 is not clearly described in the main text
   - The exact number of samples selected and the CLIP score threshold used for filtering require clarification
   - The choice of ε=0.05 for near-duplicates needs stronger justification
   - How conflicts are resolved in the 'combined-pruned' procedure when a sample falls within the similarity gap for one test set but outside for another

5. **Incomplete similarity analysis**: The paper does not adequately compare the distribution of similarity values between the pruned LAION dataset and ImageNet-Train across the full range of distances to test sets. While the closest sample distances may be comparable, LAION could potentially retain substantially more samples at intermediate distances (e.g., 5-20x more samples near the similarity gap as suggested in Table 6). This abundance of boundary samples could provide an unfair advantage on OOD benchmarks, compromising the validity of the conclusion. A more thorough analysis of the full similarity distributions would strengthen the claims.

6. **Confounding factors in pruning methodology**: When removing highly similar images, the paper simultaneously removes a specific subset of training data with potentially different properties beyond just similarity. The pruned datasets differ not only in similarity but also potentially in diversity, quality, caption quality, and representation of different domains. The mechanism by which similarity removal affects performance remains unclear—do similar images provide direct supervision, or do they help learn better generalizable feature representations? It's difficult to disentangle the effect of similarity removal from the loss of other beneficial properties of that subset.

7. **Modest but non-negligible performance drops**: The 10.8% relative performance drop on ImageNet-Sketch (from 50.21% to 44.78%) and the larger drops in the 'combined-pruned' setup (10.8% for Sketch, 4.8% for R) are non-trivial. While framed as "surprisingly high performance," these drops suggest that similarity does play a meaningful role—perhaps more substantial than the paper's narrative implies. The distinction between "not the primary driver" and "a significant contributing factor" deserves more careful discussion. Additionally, some performance differences on other benchmarks are modest (e.g., <1% for several datasets), and the paper lacks confidence intervals or statistical significance tests to determine whether these differences are meaningful.

8. **Limited model scope**: Only ViT-B/32 models are trained and evaluated. How do results generalize to larger models (ViT-L/14, ViT-g/14) that are more commonly deployed in practice? The findings may not hold for modern CLIP deployments, limiting practical relevance.

9. **Assumption violations and practical limitations**: The framework assumes ImageNet-Train ⊆ LAION-200M, but adding ImageNet-Train with synthetic captions may violate this assumption. More fundamentally, the pruning methodology requires access to test sets a priori, severely limiting applicability to truly unknown distributions. For practitioners encountering novel OOD benchmarks, this framework offers limited guidance without retraining.

## Questions for Authors

1. How sensitive are the main conclusions to the choice of similarity metric? Beyond the embedding ablations in Appendix E, have you considered alternative metrics (DINO, human perceptual judgments, or domain-specific models) to validate that findings are not specific to CLIP embeddings?

2. The 'combined-pruned' results show substantially larger performance drops (10.8% for Sketch, 4.8% for R) compared to individual test-set pruning. Can you provide detailed analysis of why combining multiple test sets leads to larger drops? Does this suggest significant overlap in the pruned sets, or are there synergistic effects between different types of similar images?

3. For the similarity gap framework,

reviews.summary: The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.
reviews.strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Summary:
The goal of this work is to understand whether the superior OOD performance of foundation models like CLIP is a result of the training dataset containing images that are very similar to the OOD test set. Towards this, the authors systematically create several splits of the base LAION dataset that was used for training OpenCLIP. Firstly, they find that pruning samples that are very similar to the OOD test sets results in a considerable drop in the OOD performance. However, by matching the train-test similarity with that of ImageNet for a fair comparison, the authors find that CLIP still shows significant gains when compared to an ImageNet pretrained model. Thus, although LAION contains images that are very similar to ImageNet-OOD test sets, this is not the key reason for better OOD generalization of CLIP. Understanding the reasons for better generalization of CLIP still remains an open question.

Strengths:
- The key finding that despite reducing the similarity between the training data and the test sets, there is an improvement in test set performance, is helpful. 
- Several insightful experimental results are presented, which is helpful for the community, especially given that these experiments are very computationally intensive. 
- The pruned datasets whose code is released, can help with further investigation on why CLIP models have better OOD performance.

Weaknesses:
- Although the results are interesting, my main concern is that the analysis is not sufficient to enable fair **OOD** testing. If the paper was about **ID** performance alone, removing train set samples that are similar to each test sample would have been sufficient, as reported in the paper. However, **OOD** implies that the **distribution** of images is unknown. So, to actually conclude that OOD evaluation is fair, all images from the test **domain** should have been removed, not only the images that are similar to every test set image. Therefore, as mentioned in the abstract, the term "out-of-distribution generalization" is still not meaningful even by training on the pruned datasets considered in the paper.
- To elaborate, if there are 5 sketches of the class "airplane" in the dataset, all images that are close to these sketches are removed. But there may be other airplane sketches, which are farther away than the closest image in ImageNet-test set, which are not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of the given classes and test **domain** are removed from the train set.
- Further, there may be other objects, such as "space shuttle"  which are not included in ImageNet, thus sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data, in addition to natural images of the same. Thus the domain "sketch" is not unseen by CLIP, and hence the evaluation is not truly OOD. 

Minor feedback: 
- Clarity of the abstract and contributions list needs improvement. It would be better to make a shorter summary of the key contributions.
- It would be better to give a different name to "generalization gap" as this term is used in a different context.

Typo:

"We provide anecdotal evidence in Fig. 1 where we choose samples from ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R and examine their nearest perceptual neighbors in LAION-400M and **ImageNet**"

Questions:
Several additional experiments are required to be done, in order to emulate a true **OOD** setting, as discussed in the weaknesses section.
For example, one should remove all "sketch" style images as well, from L-200M+IN-Train (sketch-pruned) in order to test on the ImageNet-sketch test set for a true OOD evaluation. Otherwise, other sketch images that are present in the dataset can help bridge the domain gap between sketches and natural images, leading to an unfair OOD evaluation.

6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper investigates whether CLIP's exceptional out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization performance stems primarily from high train-test similarity. The authors hypothesize that web-scale datasets like LAION-400M contain many images more similar to OOD benchmarks (e.g., ImageNet-Sketch, ImageNet-R) than any ImageNet training sample, potentially inflating apparent generalization. To test this, they introduce a "similarity gap" framework that enables systematic removal of highly similar images from LAION while matching ImageNet-Train's similarity distribution to test sets. Surprisingly, CLIP trained on these pruned datasets maintains substantial performance (e.g., 44.78% on ImageNet-Sketch vs. 49.39% baseline), suggesting that high train-test similarity alone cannot fully explain CLIP's generalization. The authors also identify a 100M core set of LAION that preserves near-full performance. While the paper successfully challenges the assumption that similarity is the primary driver of CLIP's success, the alternative explanations for what actually drives generalization remain largely unexplored.

## Strengths

1. **Novel and well-motivated research question**: The paper rigorously examines a fundamental assumption about CLIP's generalization that has not been systematically tested before. This is an important contribution to understanding foundation models and challenges common narratives about their OOD robustness.

2. **Rigorous methodology**: The similarity gap framework (Section 4.4) is mathematically formalized and elegantly designed to isolate the effect of highly similar images while controlling for dataset size and diversity differences. This principled approach enables a more fair comparison than simple similarity-based filtering would allow.

3. **Comprehensive experimental validation**: The work includes extensive experiments across multiple pruning strategies (near/far/random), diverse OOD benchmarks (six ImageNet-based datasets plus CelebA and Waterbirds), multiple embedding metrics, and ablations on non-ImageNet datasets (MNIST/SVHN). The correlation analysis in Figure 3 convincingly demonstrates that nearest-neighbor similarity predicts CLIP's performance.

4. **Insightful empirical findings**: The striking 30 percentage point gap between expected (14%) and observed (44.78%) accuracy on ImageNet-Sketch is compelling evidence that factors beyond similarity drive performance. Additionally, the observation that removing dissimilar images sometimes slightly improves performance suggests that dissimilar samples may act as noise.

5. **Reproducibility and practical contributions**: Code is released, experimental details are thorough, and the 100M LAION core set is a useful contribution that could facilitate future research while reducing computational requirements.

6. **Clear presentation**: The paper is well-written with effective visualizations (Figures 1-5) that build intuition about the problem and results.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Fundamental concerns about OOD evaluation validity**: While the paper makes progress on understanding CLIP's generalization, the core methodological concern is that the analysis does not establish truly fair OOD testing. The key issue is that OOD evaluation requires that the **distribution** of images is unknown, not merely that individual images similar to test samples are removed. To ensure fair OOD evaluation, all images from the test **domain** should be removed, not only images similar to specific test samples. For example, if there are 5 sketches of "airplane" in the dataset, removing only those images similar to these sketches leaves other airplane sketches farther away that are still not removed. Thus, it is not guaranteed that all images of given classes from the test domain are removed from the training set. Furthermore, there may be objects like "space shuttle" not included in ImageNet, meaning sketches of space shuttles can be present in the training data alongside natural images. Therefore, the domain "sketch" is not truly unseen by CLIP, and the evaluation cannot be considered genuinely OOD. Several additional experiments would be required to emulate a true OOD setting—for instance, one should remove all sketch-style images from the pruned datasets when evaluating on ImageNet-Sketch to achieve true OOD evaluation.

2. **Incomplete mechanistic understanding**: While the paper convincingly demonstrates that high train-test similarity is insufficient to explain CLIP's performance, it offers limited insight into what actually drives generalization. The discussion acknowledges scale, diversity, and density as likely factors but provides limited concrete evidence or systematic investigation of these alternatives. Compositionality is mentioned as a potential explanation (e.g., generalizing from sketches of cats to sketches of dogs) but is left largely unexplored. This leaves a significant explanatory gap—the paper tells us what CLIP's generalization is NOT caused by, but not what it IS caused by.

3. **Similarity metric dependency and potential circularity**: Although Appendix E provides ablations comparing different CLIP embeddings (ViT-B/16+ and ViT-L-14), the entire analysis hinges on using CLIP's own embedding space as the similarity metric. This creates a potential circularity: the metric for evaluating CLIP's training data is computed from the model being studied. The authors acknowledge they cannot guarantee all highly similar images were removed. Moreover, the choice of ε=0.05 for near-duplicate detection appears somewhat arbitrary. Alternative metrics (DINO, other vision models, human perceptual judgments) might yield different conclusions. While Figure 2 provides visual validation of similarity, quantitative human studies comparing embedding similarity to actual visual similarity would strengthen the claims.

4. **Limited scope of analysis**: The study focuses almost exclusively on ImageNet-based benchmarks and zero-shot classification. While Appendix D briefly explores CelebA and Waterbirds, the main narrative emphasizes ImageNet. Whether findings generalize to truly different domains (medical imaging, satellite imagery, remote sensing), other tasks (retrieval, detection, VQA), or non-vision-language models remains unclear. This substantially limits the generalizability of conclusions.

5. **Confounding factors in dataset pruning**: When removing highly similar images, the pruned datasets differ not only in similarity but also in other distributional properties—diversity, quality, representation of different domains, and potentially the types of objects included. It is unclear whether performance drops are primarily due to similarity removal or due to loss of beneficial properties of the removed subset. Dissimilar images might contribute to performance through mechanisms other than similarity (e.g., providing diverse training examples). The paper does not adequately disentangle these effects.

## Minor Weaknesses

1. **Modest performance drops on key benchmarks require nuanced interpretation**: The 10.8% relative performance drop on ImageNet-Sketch (from 50.21% to 44.78%) is non-trivial and could be interpreted as suggesting that high similarity does play a meaningful role. The framing that "high similarity is insufficient" is correct but somewhat conservative. A more nuanced discussion of the boundary between "insufficient" and "non-negligible" would be valuable. Similarly, the "combined-pruned" results show larger drops (10.8% for Sketch, 4.8% for R) than individual pruning, suggesting interactions that are not fully explored.

2. **Limited analysis of why similarity matters**: The paper demonstrates that removing similar images hurts performance, but doesn't deeply investigate the mechanism. Is it because similar images provide direct supervision? Do they help learn better feature representations? Are they essential for certain object classes or domains? The causal mechanism remains unclear.

3. **Methodological assumptions and violations**: The framework assumes ImageNet-Train ⊆ LAION-200M when formalizing the similarity gap. However, adding ImageNet-Train with synthetic captions may violate this assumption. The paper acknowledges this but does not thoroughly address its impact. Additionally, the paper uses CLIP ViT-B/32 for training but CLIP ViT-B/16+ for computing similarity—the sensitivity of results to this mismatch deserves more investigation.

4. **Statistical rigor**: No confidence intervals or statistical significance tests are provided for the reported accuracies. Some performance differences are modest (e.g., <1% for several datasets in Table 1), and it is unclear whether these differences are statistically significant. Bootstrap confidence intervals or other statistical tests would strengthen the claims.

5. **Limited evaluation on modern architectures**: Only ViT-B/32 models are trained. Larger models (ViT-L/14, ViT-g/14) that are more commonly deployed in practice are not evaluated, limiting the relevance of findings to current applications.

6. **Practical applicability concerns**: The pruning methodology requires access to test sets a priori, severely limiting applicability to truly unknown distributions. For practitioners, the framework offers limited guidance on how to identify and remove highly similar images without access to test sets.

## Questions for Authors

1. Can you provide experiments that directly address the OOD evaluation validity concerns? Specifically, could you remove all images from specific domains (e.g., all sketch-style images) rather than just those similar to test samples, and evaluate whether true OOD performance changes?

2. Have you considered using alternative similarity metrics (e.g., DINO embeddings, human perceptual judg

reviews.summary: This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.
reviews.strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?
reviews.questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

Typos:
- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider

Summary:
This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.

Strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.

Weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?

Questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

Typos:
- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper investigates a fundamental question about CLIP's exceptional out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization: does it primarily stem from high train-test similarity? The authors hypothesize that web-scale datasets like LAION-400M contain many samples more similar to OOD benchmarks than any ImageNet training sample, potentially inflating apparent generalization. They develop a "similarity gap" framework to systematically remove highly similar images from LAION such that its similarity distribution to test sets matches ImageNet-Train's distribution. Surprisingly, CLIP trained on these pruned datasets maintains substantial performance (e.g., 44.78% on ImageNet-Sketch vs. expected ~14% based on ImageNet-trained models), suggesting that high train-test similarity alone cannot explain CLIP's generalization. The work also identifies a 100M LAION subset that preserves near-full performance.

## Strengths

The paper addresses an important and timely question about foundation model generalization, rigorously examining assumptions that have been largely taken for granted. Figure 1 effectively demonstrates that LAION-400M contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while ImageNet-Train does not, providing clear initial motivation. The similarity gap framework (Section 4.4) is elegant and well-motivated, providing a principled approach to isolate the effect of highly similar images while controlling for dataset size differences—a non-trivial methodological contribution that could inspire future work.

The experimental validation is comprehensive, with extensive ablations across multiple ImageNet-based OOD benchmarks, different pruning strategies (near/far/random), and different embedding spaces (Appendix E). Figure 3 quantitatively demonstrates that nearest neighbors in LAION-400M are on average more similar than those in ImageNet-Train, and the correlation analysis convincingly shows that CLIP's performance improves on samples with similar training neighbors. Figure 4 provides compelling evidence that pruning near-similar samples causes substantial accuracy drops compared to random pruning, establishing that these similar samples are indeed important for OOD generalization.

The paper is reproducible, with code and detailed experimental descriptions provided. The practical contribution of identifying a 100M LAION core set that maintains performance could be valuable for future research and reduces computational requirements. The presentation is clear with effective visualizations that build intuition throughout.

## Major Weaknesses

**Incomplete Causal and Mechanistic Understanding**: While the paper convincingly demonstrates that high train-test similarity is *insufficient* to explain CLIP's performance, it provides limited insight into what *does* drive generalization. The discussion acknowledges scale, diversity, and density as likely factors but leaves these largely unexplored. The compositionality question—whether CLIP generalizes from sketches of cats and natural images of dogs to sketches of dogs—is mentioned as open but not investigated. This is a significant limitation: the paper tells us what CLIP's generalization is NOT caused by, but not what it IS caused by. The striking 30 percentage point gap between expected accuracy (14% based on ImageNet models) and observed accuracy (44.78%) remains largely unexplained.

**Limited Scope and Generalizability**: The analysis focuses almost exclusively on ImageNet-based OOD benchmarks and zero-shot classification. While Appendix D briefly explores CelebA and Waterbirds, and Appendix C justifies the ImageNet focus, the generalizability to truly different domains (medical imaging, satellite imagery, remote sensing) remains unclear. The paper does not evaluate whether findings extend to other vision-language models (ALIGN, BLIP) or larger CLIP architectures (ViT-L/14, ViT-H) commonly deployed in practice. Only ViT-B/32 is trained, despite larger models being more relevant to modern CLIP deployments. The restriction to zero-shot classification also leaves open whether findings apply to few-shot or fine-tuning scenarios.

**Insufficient Experimental Evidence for Key Claims**: The paper does not definitively establish that near-pruned samples are crucial specifically for OOD generalization rather than general image-classification performance. Two critical experiments are missing: (i) demonstrating that pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch only degrades performance on ImageNet-Sketch, not other benchmarks; (ii) showing whether CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than ImageNet-trained models when pruned in analogous ways. Without the latter comparison, we cannot determine whether the observed effects are specific to CLIP's training paradigm or reflect general properties of large-scale training. Additionally, the "combined-pruned" experiment shows larger performance drops (10.8% for Sketch, 4.8% for R) than individual pruning, suggesting important interactions that are not fully explored. Why does simultaneous pruning for multiple test sets cause larger drops?

**Similarity Metric Dependency and Potential Circularity**: While Appendix E provides ablations comparing CLIP ViT-B/16+ and ViT-L-14 embeddings, the entire analysis depends on using CLIP's embedding space as the similarity metric. This creates potential circularity—the metric is derived from the model being studied. The authors acknowledge they cannot guarantee all highly similar images were removed. The choice of threshold ε=0.05 for near-duplicates appears somewhat arbitrary, and the paper does not thoroughly explore sensitivity to this choice. Beyond embedding ablations, the paper lacks quantitative human perceptual studies validating that embedding similarity correlates with actual visual similarity. Using CLIP embeddings to evaluate CLIP's own training data introduces a dependency that could bias results in ways not fully characterized.

**Confounded Effects in Pruning**: When removing highly similar images, the method also removes a specific subset of training data with potentially different properties beyond similarity—including diversity, quality, representation of different domains, and other distributional characteristics. The performance drops could reflect loss of beneficial properties of that subset rather than purely the effect of similarity removal. The observation that far-pruning (removing dissimilar images) sometimes slightly improves performance suggests that dissimilar images act as noise, complicating the interpretation. The paper does not adequately disentangle whether drops are due to similarity removal or loss of other beneficial properties.

## Minor Weaknesses

**Conceptual and Practical Limitations**: The paper does not adequately address fundamental questions about what constitutes "zero-shot" learning and "out-of-distribution" robustness. After showing that pruning degrades OOD generalization, it remains unclear what the community should do with these findings. Should we say CLIP models have "cheated" by seeing similar samples? The motivation for Sections 5 and 6—"correcting" for highly similar images by pruning—is unclear. Why would we want models that perform worse on benchmarks if exact test samples do not appear in the training set? The pruning methodology also requires access to test sets a priori, severely limiting applicability to truly unknown distributions. This transductive learning concern is not adequately addressed, and the paper does not discuss potential violations of dataset licenses (e.g., ObjectNet's prohibition on using it to tune model parameters).

**Statistical Rigor**: No confidence intervals or statistical significance tests are provided for reported accuracies. Some performance differences are modest (e.g., <1% for several datasets in Table 1, 4.8% on ImageNet-R), and it is unclear whether these are statistically significant. This is particularly important given the modest drops on some benchmarks.

**Framing and Interpretation**: The paper frames the 10.8% relative performance drop on ImageNet-Sketch as "surprisingly high," supporting the claim that high similarity is insufficient. However, one could argue this is substantial and suggests similarity plays a meaningful role. The distinction between "insufficient" and "non-negligible" deserves clearer articulation. The claim that highly similar images are "clearly a factor" based on visualizations should be quantified more rigorously—what fraction of the performance gap is explained by factors other than similarity?

**Methodological Assumptions**: The assumption in Section 4.4 that ImageNet-Train ⊆ LAION-200M may not hold perfectly, as ImageNet-Train is added with synthetic captions. How does this affect the validity of comparisons? The paper acknowledges but does not thoroughly address this issue. Additionally, how sensitive are results to the choice of ImageNet-Train as the reference point? Would using a different reference dataset (e.g., random natural images) substantially change conclusions?

## Technical Issues and Questions

1. **Similarity metric validation**: Beyond embedding ablations, can you provide quantitative validation that CLIP embedding similarity correlates with human perceptual similarity? Could alternative metrics (DINO, domain-specific models) substantially change pruning decisions?

2. **Combined-pruned analysis**: The larger performance drops in combined-pruned setup suggest important interactions. Can you provide detailed analysis of overlap between pruned sets and characterize what makes combined pruning qualitatively different?

3. **Threshold sensitivity**: How sensitive are conclusions to the choice of ε=0.05 for near-duplicates? Is there a sharp transition or gradual degradation with different thresholds?

4. **Model and architecture generalization**: How
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the hypothesis “Does CLIP’s accuracy on test sets mainly stem from highly similar images in its train set?”. Their approach is to take a CLIP training dataset, LAION, and remove samples similar to OOD benchmarks, then retrain and evaluate the performance drop. In Section 4.1, they show that for some OOD datasets e.g., ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-R, there are more similar images to the OOD dataset in LAION than there are in the ImageNet-train set. These images are both semantically and stylistically more similar. They also observe a positive correlation between accuracy and having similar neighbors in the training set for each OOD benchmark. In the remainder of the paper, they prune the dataset by removing similar samples to OOD datasets and evaluate the performance of models trained on pruned datasets.

Strengths:
- Figure 1 clearly shows that the LAION-400M dataset contains semantically and stylistically similar images to OOD benchmarks while the ImageNet training set does not.
- Figure 3 quantitatively shows the nearest neighbors of OOD datasets in LAION-400M are on average more similar than the nearest neighbors from ImageNet-train set except for the Imagenet-Val set itself. Fig 3.right also clearly shows that CLIP model performs better on samples that it has seen similar images of it in the training set.
- Figure 4 shows that pruning LAION for similar samples to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val results in substantial accuracy drop compared with pruning random samples. Hence showing that these similar samples are crucial for the effective robustness of CLIP models.

Weaknesses:
- Even though the results in Figure 4 show the near-pruned samples are crucial for OOD generalization of CLIP, they are not conclusive. In particular, the following two experiments would be useful:
i) Does a model *only* lose OOD generalization on one benchmark or are these near-pruned samples crucial for all sorts of image-classification performance on any benchmark? This requires plotting the accuracy on two datasets, e.g., ImageNet-Val and ImageNet-Sketch, and showing that after pruning samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch, we only lose performance on ImageNet-Sketch.
ii) Do CLIP models lose OOD generalization more quickly than classification models trained on ImageNet? For this, one would prune ImageNet with a similar procedure for X% of samples and compare whether the accuracy drop in ImageNet models is slower than CLIP models. If not, then we would know that any OOD generalization we have been seeing could be due to seeing similar samples in the training set.

- This paper is a good place to have a broader discussion on “What is zero-shot?” and “What is out-of-distribution robustness?” and I think the paper should expand on that. In particular, after showing that pruning loses OOD generalization, it is not clear what the community should do with these datasets and evaluation benchmarks. Should we say CLIP models have cheated and they are not zero-shot? Related to that, the objective of Sections 5 and 6 that “Correct for highly similar images” is not clear. Why would we want to prune our training datasets to get worse on some test benchmarks if those exact test samples do not appear in the training set? Why would we want models in Table 1 trained on pruned datasets that perform worse?

Questions:
- Figure 4: What if we perform a similar process for training on the ImageNet dataset? Do we observe a similar accuracy drop?
- Figure 7 shows that removing samples similar to ImageNet-Sketch and ImageNet-Val result in lower performance in all other ImageNet OOD datasets as well. Could this mean that these samples are important for a general understanding of the ImageNet distribution?
- Section 5: Would this method and any model trained on this dataset be considered as transductive learning? Because the test datasets would have been seen directly or indirectly by the model. Would this be against the license of datasets such as ObjectNet that say “ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model.”?

Typos:
- Page 7: Let us for consider -> Let us consider

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Simple Hierarchical Planning with Diffusion

reviews.summary: The paper proposes Hierarchical Diffuser (HD), a sub-goal based method for planning with diffusion models. In recent literature, Diffuser [1] was proposed to plan an optimal sequence of state and action between initial state and goal. Following diffuser, the authors design two diffusers. One diffuser plans for sub-goals between the start and the goal state. For each sub-goal segment, the second diffuser plans the optimal state-action sequence. Experiments are conducted on Maze, AntMaze, MuJoCo Gym and FrankKitchen benchmarks. The performance of HD is compared with relevant offline RL and hierarchical RL algorithms. Further, the experiments show improved receptibility, OOD generalization and less computation overhead as compared to diffuser. Relevant ablations are performed.
reviews.strengths:
Discretizing planning into sub-goals with diffusion models is shown to be advantageous as more diverse scenarios can be solved. Low-level diffusion planning becomes task-agnostic.

Even the sparse diffuser or SD version is better than diffuser because of increased receptive field. The authors validate this by showing that increasing the kernel-size (hence the receptive field) of diffuser leads to better performance but weaker generalization.

SD with dense actions leads to better fitting of the sparse objective, a typical bottleneck in hierarchical RL.

HD takes lesser time than Diffuser due to shorter high-level sequence and parallelly solving low-level plans for all the segments.
reviews.weaknesses:
The formulation has limited novelty. While a single diffuser is not sufficient for planning over long-horizons, the work introduces two diffusers: one sparse diffuser for planning sequence of sub-goals between initial state and goal, while a standard diffuser solves for individual sub-goal segments.

Given that there are also methods which perform state-only diffusion like Decision-diffuser [2], is it possible to perform relevant ablations to justify why states are not sufficient for having a good estimate of $J$? If you are following diffuser’s codebase, are you using the diffusion sampled actions or a separate controller for Maze2d tasks like diffuser?

Solving for sub-goals independently without the knowledge about the final goal might lead to non-optimal behavior. How do you ensure that the sampled path for one segment does not overlap with the paths for the other segment? How many trajectories do you sample for individual segments and for the sub-goal sequence?

Also, what happens when actions do not lead to the exact sub-goal (unless you use inverse dynamics actions) for the maze case? Because there is no-feedback from the low-level diffuser to high-level diffuser, it might become challenging.

Overall, I acknowledge that the presented method is a promising revision of Diffuser which performs better in all aspects. However, I believe that the contributions are not significant enough.
reviews.questions: See weakness above.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
