Summary:
The paper studies the problem out-of-distribution (OOD) detection when original in-distribution (ID) data suffers from class imbalance. The authors first report an experimental observation regarding two major challenges in OOD detection under the studied setting: misidentification of tail-side ID samples as OOD (False positives) and of some OOD samples as head-side ID (False negatives). They then statistically compare the learning objectives in OOD detection with class-balanced vs. -imbalanced ID data, allowing them to attribute these challenges to the auxiliary bias term that does not cancel out in the class-imbalanced scenario. Based on this analysis, they propose two unique avenues to mitigate the influence of this bias term: post-hoc normalization and train-time regularization. Together, they form a cohesive framework for OOD detection with class-imbalanced ID data, dubbed ImOOD. The authors provide substantial empirical evidence for the effectiveness of ImOOD.

Strengths:
- The statistical analysis performed in the paper is sound and written in a manner that is easy to follow. The authors include a helpful intuitive understanding of the bias term that connects it back to the empirical observations about the behavior of OOD detection models under the class-imbalanced setting.
- ImOOD is well-motivated and theoretically-grounded on the the above analysis. The general thinking behind the method is sound, and for the most part, it’s described clearly enough to understand and re-implement the design.

Weaknesses:
- I believe the major drawback of this paper lies in the limited scope of the studied problem. It appears that the authors assume that the class-imbalance problem only exists on the original ID dataset. I am not too convinced with the practicality of the proposed scenario; it’s hard to imagine in what deployment scenarios only the ID dataset will suffer from this problem, while the OOD dataset(s) used for training/testing is void of it. Wouldn’t it be more natural to assume that the ID training dataset can be refined in advance to make it class-balanced, while the OOD data the detector must be able to identify arise in various forms, and thus is more likely to be class-imbalanced?
    - What if the auxiliary OOD dataset used for training and/or the target OOD dataset exhibits class-imbalance as well? I think it is important to study various combinations of class-balanced and -imbalanced ID/auxiliary OOD/target OOD datasets. Does the analysis performed in the paper extend to and hold in such settings? Can ImOOD still outperform other baselines?
- Please correct me if I am wrong, but it appears that the verification of post-hoc normalization (minus the train-time regularization) on larger datasets appears to be missing. Even on CIFAR10-LT, the improvement from post-hoc normalization seems marginal, but this concern could be alleviated, as long as the improvement is consistent across various datasets. If post-hoc normalization is not quite as effective on datasets, it would signify that ImOOD is heavily reliant on the train-time regularization.
- More details on how the label distribution is learned (during test-time regularization) would be appreciated. Also, do you use the same learned label distribution when performing post-hoc normalization? Or is it used only for training-time regularization? If you discard it after training, one would have to know the label distribution anyways for post-hoc normalization, so do we really need to use the learned distribution in the first place?
- The empirical validation is limited to one target OOD dataset per InD dataset. Validation on more challenging OOD datasets (e.g., near OOD data, data with spurious correlation) would be helpful to gauge the effectiveness of the proposed method.

Questions:
Please refer to the Weaknesses section.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review: Out-of-Distribution Detection on Imbalanced Data

## Summary

This paper addresses out-of-distribution (OOD) detection in the practical setting where the in-distribution (ID) training data suffers from class imbalance. The authors identify two complementary failure modes: misidentification of tail-class ID samples as OOD (false positives) and misclassification of OOD samples as head-class ID (false negatives). Through statistical analysis, they derive a class-aware bias term β(x) = Σ_y P(y|x,i)/P(y) that explains the performance gap between OOD detection on balanced versus imbalanced ID data. Based on this insight, they propose ImOOD, a framework comprising two techniques: (1) post-hoc normalization (Eq. 9) that adjusts OOD scores using classification probabilities and class priors, and (2) training-time regularization (Eq. 10) with learnable class priors. Experiments on CIFAR10-LT, CIFAR100-LT, and ImageNet-LT demonstrate consistent improvements over prior work, particularly PASCL.

## Strengths

The paper makes several noteworthy contributions. First, it clearly articulates and visualizes a practical problem that has received limited attention in the literature. The empirical observations in Figure 1 provide concrete motivation for why existing OOD detectors fail under class imbalance, and the connection between these failure modes and the underlying bias term is intuitive and well-explained.

Second, the statistical analysis is sound and rigorously presented. The derivation of the bias term β(x) from Bayesian principles (Eqs. 5-8) is mathematically correct and provides clear intuition for practitioners. The authors do an excellent job connecting the theoretical analysis back to empirical observations, making the work accessible and well-motivated. The framework elegantly unifies two seemingly different approaches—post-hoc normalization and training-time regularization—under a single principled perspective.

Third, the experimental validation is comprehensive and demonstrates generalizability. The method is evaluated across three large-scale benchmarks with multiple OOD detection baselines (OE, Energy, BinDisc), and ablation studies (Table 4) effectively validate design choices. The error distribution analysis in Figures A1-A2 provides useful insights into how the method addresses the identified problems. Implementation details are provided for reproducibility, following established experimental protocols.

## Major Weaknesses

### Limited Scope and Practical Assumptions

The most significant limitation concerns the scope of the studied problem. The paper assumes that class imbalance affects only the original ID training dataset, while the OOD datasets used for both training and testing remain class-balanced. This assumption warrants scrutiny from a practical standpoint. In real-world deployment scenarios, it is difficult to envision situations where only the ID dataset suffers from class imbalance while auxiliary OOD data and target OOD data remain perfectly balanced. A more natural scenario might be one where the ID training dataset can be refined in advance to achieve class balance, while OOD data—arising from diverse sources in deployment—is inherently class-imbalanced.

The paper would be substantially strengthened by studying various combinations of class-balanced and -imbalanced ID, auxiliary OOD, and target OOD datasets. Does the theoretical analysis extend to these more realistic scenarios? Can ImOOD still outperform baselines when the OOD data itself exhibits class imbalance? This gap significantly limits the practical applicability of the work.

### Insufficient Justification for Learnable Priors

A critical weakness lies in the motivation for learning class priors πy(θ) rather than using empirical frequencies ˆπy. The paper claims that empirical priors are "sensitive to n_o" and "not precise," but this justification is vague and lacks principled grounding. The analogy to "prompt tuning" (Section 3.3) feels forced and does not adequately explain why gradient-based optimization of priors should yield better estimates than empirical frequencies. 

Notably, Figure 2 reveals that learned priors are often very similar to empirical ones, raising fundamental questions about the necessity of this component. Why should learning these parameters through gradient descent be theoretically sound? Does it converge to meaningful estimates of P(y)? The ablation study in Table 4 demonstrates empirical improvements but does not illuminate the underlying mechanism. Without stronger theoretical or empirical justification, this design choice appears to be a heuristic fix rather than a principled solution. The paper would benefit from convergence analysis, sensitivity studies to initialization, or deeper investigation into when and why learned priors diverge from empirical estimates.

### Limited Novelty of Core Theoretical Insight

While the statistical framework is rigorous, the core theoretical contribution is relatively incremental. The bias term β(x)—essentially an inverse class prior reweighting—is a well-established technique in imbalanced learning literature. The main contribution amounts to applying this known principle to the OOD detection setting, which, while useful, lacks surprising conceptual novelty. The derivation in Section 3.1, though correct, is straightforward algebra and does not provide deep insights into when or why the bias term matters, how large it typically is across different imbalance ratios, or the sample complexity for learning class priors. The connection to Bayesian principles, while present, is relatively informal.

The distinction from concurrent work (e.g., Jiang et al., 2023) who also use class priors for post-hoc normalization remains unclear. This comparison, relegated to the appendix, complicates assessment of the paper's novelty.

### Incomplete Problem Solution and Modest Empirical Gains

Figures A1 and A2 acknowledge that the imbalance problem is "not fully solved," yet the paper does not investigate why this occurs or propose solutions beyond the current approach. This incompleteness weakens the contribution's scope. More concerning, the post-hoc normalization component (Table 1) yields only marginal improvements—often 0.3-0.5% AUROC—suggesting that the simple bias correction has limited practical impact. The main gains come from training-time regularization, which is less novel. This raises the question: Is the bias term β(x) actually the primary source of performance degradation, or is the post-hoc correction simply insufficient?

The improvements on standard benchmarks are also mixed. While ImageNet-LT shows stronger gains (5.2% AUROC), improvements on CIFAR10-LT and CIFAR100-LT are modest (1.7% and 0.8-1.7% respectively). Some improvements lack clear statistical significance—Table 1 shows overlapping error bars for certain metrics (e.g., 0.3% for OE), and the paper does not provide significance testing. The comparison with BalEnergy (Choi et al., 2023) is relegated to the appendix with different experimental settings, making fair comparison difficult. The substantial variation in improvements across benchmarks (1.7% on CIFAR10-LT vs. 5.2% on ImageNet-LT) is attributed post-hoc to model capacity differences (ResNet18 vs ResNet50), but this explanation lacks rigorous analysis and may reflect confounds in the experimental setup rather than fundamental properties of the method.

## Minor Weaknesses

### Incomplete Experimental Analysis

Several experimental gaps limit confidence in the results:

1. **Post-hoc normalization verification**: Verification of post-hoc normalization (independent of training-time regularization) appears limited, particularly on larger datasets. Even on CIFAR10-LT, improvements are marginal. Consistent validation across diverse datasets would strengthen claims about the method's effectiveness and whether ImOOD is heavily reliant on training-time regularization.

2. **Auxiliary OOD data sensitivity**: The method requires auxiliary OOD training data, but sensitivity to the choice and quantity of such data is not thoroughly explored. How do results vary with different auxiliary OOD datasets (e.g., TinyImages vs. other sources)? How does performance degrade with limited OOD samples? This is a practical limitation that deserves more investigation.

3. **Limited OOD diversity**: Empirical validation is restricted to one target OOD dataset per ID dataset. Evaluation on more challenging and diverse OOD scenarios—including near-OOD data, semantically similar distributions, and data with spurious correlations—would better gauge the method's robustness and generalizability.

4. **Imbalance ratio exploration**: The paper focuses on imbalance ratio ρ=100. Do results hold for other imbalance ratios or non-uniform distributions? This narrow scope limits understanding of the method's applicability across different imbalance settings.

### Unclear Technical Details

1. **Label distribution learning**: The paper provides limited detail on how the label distribution is learned during training-time regularization. Is the learned distribution used only for training-time regularization, or is it also employed during post-hoc normalization at test time? If the distribution is discarded after
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper studies the problem out-of-distribution (OOD) detection when original in-distribution (ID) data suffers from class imbalance. The authors first report an experimental observation regarding two major challenges in OOD detection under the studied setting: misidentification of tail-side ID samples as OOD (False positives) and of some OOD samples as head-side ID (False negatives). They then statistically compare the learning objectives in OOD detection with class-balanced vs. -imbalanced ID data, allowing them to attribute these challenges to the auxiliary bias term that does not cancel out in the class-imbalanced scenario. Based on this analysis, they propose two unique avenues to mitigate the influence of this bias term: post-hoc normalization and train-time regularization. Together, they form a cohesive framework for OOD detection with class-imbalanced ID data, dubbed ImOOD. The authors provide substantial empirical evidence for the effectiveness of ImOOD.

Strengths:
- The statistical analysis performed in the paper is sound and written in a manner that is easy to follow. The authors include a helpful intuitive understanding of the bias term that connects it back to the empirical observations about the behavior of OOD detection models under the class-imbalanced setting.
- ImOOD is well-motivated and theoretically-grounded on the the above analysis. The general thinking behind the method is sound, and for the most part, it’s described clearly enough to understand and re-implement the design.

Weaknesses:
- I believe the major drawback of this paper lies in the limited scope of the studied problem. It appears that the authors assume that the class-imbalance problem only exists on the original ID dataset. I am not too convinced with the practicality of the proposed scenario; it’s hard to imagine in what deployment scenarios only the ID dataset will suffer from this problem, while the OOD dataset(s) used for training/testing is void of it. Wouldn’t it be more natural to assume that the ID training dataset can be refined in advance to make it class-balanced, while the OOD data the detector must be able to identify arise in various forms, and thus is more likely to be class-imbalanced?
    - What if the auxiliary OOD dataset used for training and/or the target OOD dataset exhibits class-imbalance as well? I think it is important to study various combinations of class-balanced and -imbalanced ID/auxiliary OOD/target OOD datasets. Does the analysis performed in the paper extend to and hold in such settings? Can ImOOD still outperform other baselines?
- Please correct me if I am wrong, but it appears that the verification of post-hoc normalization (minus the train-time regularization) on larger datasets appears to be missing. Even on CIFAR10-LT, the improvement from post-hoc normalization seems marginal, but this concern could be alleviated, as long as the improvement is consistent across various datasets. If post-hoc normalization is not quite as effective on datasets, it would signify that ImOOD is heavily reliant on the train-time regularization.
- More details on how the label distribution is learned (during test-time regularization) would be appreciated. Also, do you use the same learned label distribution when performing post-hoc normalization? Or is it used only for training-time regularization? If you discard it after training, one would have to know the label distribution anyways for post-hoc normalization, so do we really need to use the learned distribution in the first place?
- The empirical validation is limited to one target OOD dataset per InD dataset. Validation on more challenging OOD datasets (e.g., near OOD data, data with spurious correlation) would be helpful to gauge the effectiveness of the proposed method.

Questions:
Please refer to the Weaknesses section.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

Summary:
When encountering inherent imbalance of in-distribution (ID) data, the paper identified two common challenges faced by different OOD detectors: misidentifying tail class ID samples as OOD, while erroneously predicting OOD samples as head class from ID. To explain this phenomenon, the authors introduce a generalized statistical framework, termed ImOOD, to formulate the OOD detection problem on imbalanced data distribution. Consequently, the theoretical analysis reveals that there exists a class-aware bias item between balanced and imbalanced OOD detection, which contributes to the performance gap.

Strengths:
1. The paper is written well and is easy to understand.
2. The studied problem is very important.
3. The results seem to outperform state-of-the-art.

Weaknesses:
1. I am curious about the possibility of this method being extended to distance-based OOD detection methods, such as Mahalanobios distance, etc. How well does the current method compare to the state-of-the-art distance-based methods?

reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review: Out-of-Distribution Detection on Imbalanced Data

## Summary

This paper addresses an important and practical problem: out-of-distribution (OOD) detection under class-imbalanced in-distribution data. The authors identify two complementary failure modes of existing OOD detectors—misidentifying tail-class ID samples as OOD while erroneously predicting OOD samples as head-class ID—and propose ImOOD, a statistical framework to explain and mitigate this problem. The key contribution is the derivation of a class-aware bias term β(x) = Σ_y P(y|x,i)/P(y) that characterizes the performance gap between balanced and imbalanced OOD detection. Based on this analysis, the paper proposes two complementary techniques: (1) post-hoc normalization via Eq. (9) that adjusts OOD scores using classification probabilities and class priors, and (2) training-time regularization via Eq. (10) with learnable class priors. Experiments on CIFAR10-LT, CIFAR100-LT, and ImageNet-LT demonstrate consistent improvements over the PASCL baseline, with gains ranging from 1.7% to 5.2% AUROC across benchmarks.

## Strengths

1. **Well-motivated problem and clear presentation**: The paper articulates a concrete, practically important problem that has received limited attention. Figure 1 provides compelling empirical evidence of the two failure modes through statistical visualization, and the overall presentation is clear and easy to follow. The problem formulation naturally arises in real-world deployments where data imbalance is ubiquitous.

2. **Principled theoretical framework**: The derivation of the bias term β(x) from Bayesian principles (Eqs. 5-8) is mathematically sound and provides clear intuition for why imbalanced data degrades OOD detection performance. The connection between balanced accuracy correction from imbalanced classification and OOD detection is well-established and rigorously derived.

3. **Generalizability across methods**: The proposed approach is versatile and can be applied to multiple OOD detection methods (OE, Energy, BinDisc) in both post-hoc and training-time variants, demonstrating broad applicability. The unified framework connecting both approaches is conceptually coherent.

4. **Comprehensive experimental validation**: The evaluation spans three large-scale benchmarks (CIFAR10-LT, CIFAR100-LT, ImageNet-LT) with multiple OOD test sets and baseline methods. Ablation studies in Table 4 effectively validate design choices, and error distribution analysis (Figures A1-A2) provides useful insights into how the method addresses the identified problems. The detailed implementation details enhance reproducibility.

5. **Consistent empirical improvements**: The method shows consistent gains over the PASCL baseline across all benchmarks, with particularly strong improvements on ImageNet-LT (5.2% AUROC), suggesting practical value for practitioners working with imbalanced datasets.

## Weaknesses

1. **Limited theoretical novelty**: While the bias term derivation is rigorous, the core insight—adjusting for class priors via dividing by P(y)—is relatively straightforward and represents a direct application of well-established balanced accuracy correction techniques from imbalanced learning to the OOD detection setting. The theoretical analysis, though correct, is essentially standard algebra and does not provide surprising new insights into when or why the bias term matters. Notably, the connection to Bayesian principles is somewhat informal, and the paper lacks theoretical analysis of how classification errors under severe imbalance propagate to the OOD detection framework or analysis of convergence properties for the proposed approach.

2. **Questionable justification for learnable priors**: A significant weakness is the switch from empirical class priors ˆπ_y to learnable parameters πy(θ) in Eq. (10). The motivation invoking "prompt tuning" is somewhat forced and not well justified theoretically. The paper argues that ˆπ_y is "sensitive to n_o" and "not precise," but provides no principled explanation for why gradient-based learning of πy should solve these issues better than empirical estimation. More critically, Figure 2 reveals that learned priors are often very similar to empirical ones, raising fundamental questions about whether this added complexity is necessary. The ablation in Table 4 shows improvements, but without understanding the mechanism, it appears to be a heuristic fix rather than a principled solution. Additionally, there is no analysis of convergence properties, sensitivity to initialization, or what prevents πy from diverging during training.

3. **Modest post-hoc improvements reveal incomplete solution**: The post-hoc normalization approach (Eq. 9) yields only marginal gains (0.3-0.5% AUROC in Table 1), suggesting that the identified bias term β(x) may not be the primary source of performance degradation. This raises questions about whether the theoretical analysis fully captures the problem. Furthermore, Figures A1-A2 explicitly acknowledge that the imbalance problem is "not fully solved" even after applying the proposed methods, yet the paper provides no investigation into why this occurs or what fundamental limits exist. The authors attribute this to scarce tail-class training data but do not propose solutions, leaving the contribution incomplete.

4. **Practical limitations regarding auxiliary OOD data**: The method requires auxiliary OOD training data, which is acknowledged as a limitation but not thoroughly explored experimentally. The paper does not conduct sensitivity analysis on the choice and quantity of auxiliary OOD datasets (TinyImages80M for CIFAR, ImageNet-Extra for ImageNet), which may significantly impact results. In practical scenarios with limited or mismatched OOD samples, performance degradation is unknown. This substantially limits the method's applicability compared to OOD detectors that do not require auxiliary data.

5. **Incomplete experimental analysis and fairness concerns**: 
   - The comparison with concurrent work (BalEnergy by Choi et al., 2023) is relegated to the appendix with different experimental settings, complicating fair assessment of relative performance and showing only marginal improvements (0.3% on CIFAR10-LT).
   - Statistical significance testing is absent despite some small improvements on certain metrics (e.g., 0.3% AUROC for OE in Table 1), where error bars may overlap.
   - Limited analysis of when and why learned priors differ from empirical ones (Figure 2) and what patterns emerge across datasets.
   - The substantial variation in improvements across benchmarks (1.7% on CIFAR10-LT vs. 5.2% on ImageNet-LT) is attributed post-hoc to model capacity differences (ResNet18 vs ResNet50), but this explanation lacks rigorous justification and may reflect confounds in the experimental setup rather than fundamental properties of the method.

6. **Unaddressed questions about method design**:
   - How does the method perform when the classifier f poorly estimates P(y|x,i) due to miscalibration on tail classes, which is common under severe imbalance?
   - In Eq. (9), how is the offset log(Σ_y p_{y|x,i}/ˆπ_y) computed for test samples? Does this require access to the full test distribution, affecting practical applicability?
   - The paper claims the method "does not conflict" with feature-level approaches like PASCL (Table 5: +ImOOD+PASCL), but this appears to be a simple combination without principled integration.

7. **Limited scope of analysis**: The paper focuses exclusively on long-tailed distributions with imbalance ratio ρ=100 and does not explore robustness to other imbalance ratios or non-uniform distributions. The analysis of failure cases is minimal, and there is no discussion of scenarios where the method performs poorly or fundamental limitations.

## Questions and Suggestions

1. **Learnable priors mechanism**: Can you provide theoretical analysis or empirical evidence demonstrating when and why πy(θ) outperforms ˆπ_y? What is the interpretation when learned priors diverge substantially from empirical frequencies? Have you tested sensitivity to initialization or compared against robust prior estimation methods from the imbalanced learning literature?

2. **Bias term magnitude and significance**: Can you quantify how large β(x) typically is across different imbalance ratios and datasets? This would clarify the practical significance of the correction and help explain why post-hoc improvements are modest.

3. **Comparison with related work**: How does ImOOD differ fundamentally from Jiang et al. (2023), who also employ class priors for post-hoc normalization? The distinction remains unclear and deserves elaboration. Additionally, have you compared against logit adjustment techniques (Menon et al., 2021) adapted to OOD detection?

4. **Distance-based OOD detectors**: The paper focuses on score-based methods. How does the framework extend to distance-

reviews.summary: The main contribution of this paper is the introduction of a generalized statistical framework called ImOOD, which addresses the problem of detecting and rejecting unknown out-of-distribution (OOD) samples in the presence of imbalanced data distributions. The paper identifies two common challenges faced by existing OOD detection methods: misclassifying tail class in-distribution (ID) samples as OOD, and incorrectly predicting OOD samples as head class ID samples. Then, the authors propose a general framework that can lead to improved detection performance.
reviews.strengths:
The paper introduces a generalised statistical framework called ImOOD, which addresses the problem of OOD detection in the presence of imbalanced data distributions. Imbalanced data distributions widely exist in real world, and a set of fundamental works have proposed to tackle this questions to boost OOD detection. It seems that there may still exist many open questions, making the research direction in this paper promising in OOD detection. 

The paper identifies two common challenges faced by existing OOD detection methods, namely misclassifying tail class ID samples as OOD and incorrectly predicting OOD samples as head class ID samples. This identification helps in understanding the limitations of current approaches.

The proposed method  is driven by the Bayesian analysis, demonstrating the impacts of data imbalance and suggesting a general framework that can handle the distribution shift in OOD detection. The proposed framework, as claimed by the authors, can be used to handle imbalanced issue for a set of different scoring strategies, and the evaluation results further verify the power of their method against imbalance data more or less.
reviews.weaknesses:
A direct question is that if we have used learning algorithms that can handle imbalanced data (which is often the case in reality) to train the basic model, do we need to handle imbalanced data for OOD detection thereafter. 

The authors identify two cases that make previous OOD detection methods fail, i.e., misclassifying tail class ID samples as OOD and incorrectly predicting OOD samples as head class ID samples. It is a direct conclusion and seemingly to be important, but could the authors further connect these two cases to previous works that handle imbalanced data in OOD detection and the proposed ImOOD. For example, why previous works, such as [1], will fail to discern these two cases and why ImOOD can overcome previous drawbacks. 

g should be defined in a proper position in advance. It seems that g should be the detector built upon f.

My main concern lies in the inaccurate estimation of beta, consisting of three terms that are all biased from my view. For P(i|X), a direct failure case is that when we have a detector whose g always greater than 0 (e.g., for distance based scoring), then P(i|X) will be always greater than 0.5. Thus, all data points will be taken as ID cases, making the estimation obviously a biased case. P(y|x,i) also suffers from biased estimation, due to the well known calibration failure of deep models. Such an issue, from my view, cannot be ignored in the field of OOD detection, since it is one of the main reason why MSP score is not effective in practice. The estimation of P(y) is also biased, since the training time data distribution (ID + OOD) is different from the test situation, thus n_o used for training cannot cover the real test situation. 


Detailed discussion about the hyper-parameters setting and the choice of auxiliary OOD data and the evaluation datasets should be discussed in detailed. More methods that handle OOD detection with imbalanced data should be considered here, such as [1]. More ablation studies to test the respective power of P(i|x), P(y), and P(y|x) should be tested.


[1] Detecting Out-of-distribution Data through In-distribution Class Prior.
reviews.questions: Please see the Weaknesses.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review: ImOOD - Out-of-Distribution Detection on Imbalanced Data

## Summary

This paper introduces ImOOD, a statistical framework for detecting out-of-distribution (OOD) samples in the presence of imbalanced in-distribution (ID) data. The authors identify two complementary failure modes of existing OOD detectors: misclassifying tail-class ID samples as OOD, and incorrectly predicting OOD samples as head-class ID samples. They propose that a class-aware bias term β(x) = Σ_y P(y|x,i)/P(y) explains the performance gap between OOD detection on balanced versus imbalanced data. Based on this analysis, the paper develops two approaches: (1) post-hoc normalization via Eq. (9) that adjusts OOD scores using classification probabilities and class priors, and (2) training-time regularization via Eq. (10) that learns class priors as parameters. Experiments on CIFAR10-LT, CIFAR100-LT, and ImageNet-LT demonstrate improvements over the PASCL baseline.

## Strengths

The paper addresses a practically important and well-motivated problem. Imbalanced data distributions are ubiquitous in real-world applications, and the identification of two concrete failure modes (visualized clearly in Figure 1) provides good intuition for why existing methods struggle. The theoretical framework is mathematically sound, deriving the bias term β(x) from Bayesian principles and establishing a clear connection between balanced accuracy correction in imbalanced classification and OOD detection. The proposed method demonstrates versatility, generalizing across multiple OOD detection strategies (OE, Energy, BinDisc) and offering both post-hoc and training-time variants.

The experimental validation is comprehensive, spanning three large-scale benchmarks with multiple OOD test sets, detailed ablation studies (particularly Table 4 validating learnable priors), and error distribution analysis (Figures A1-A2) that effectively demonstrates how the method addresses the identified failure modes. The paper shows consistent improvements over the PASCL baseline, with gains of 1.7% AUROC on CIFAR10-LT and notably 5.2% on ImageNet-LT. Implementation details are thoroughly documented, supporting reproducibility.

## Major Weaknesses

### 1. Limited Theoretical Novelty and Incremental Contribution

The core insight—adjusting OOD scores by dividing by class priors P(y)—is relatively straightforward and represents a direct application of well-established balanced accuracy correction from imbalanced learning to OOD detection. The derivation in Section 3.1, while mathematically rigorous, amounts to standard algebraic manipulation of Bayes' rule and does not provide surprising theoretical insights. The connection to balanced accuracy (Eq. 3) is well-known in the imbalanced learning literature, and the extension to OOD detection, though useful, feels incremental rather than providing fundamental new insights. This raises the question of whether the contribution is substantial enough for a top-tier venue.

### 2. Critical Issues with Bias Term Estimation

The main concern involves the inaccurate estimation of the three components comprising β(x), each of which suffers from systematic bias:

**P(i|X) Estimation**: A direct failure case occurs when the detector g always produces values greater than 0 (e.g., in distance-based scoring methods). Under such conditions, P(i|X) will always exceed 0.5, causing all data points to be classified as ID samples. This fundamentally breaks the bias correction mechanism and suggests the framework may not generalize well across different scoring strategies despite claims of generality.

**P(y|x,i) Estimation**: This term suffers from well-known calibration failure of deep neural networks, a critical issue in OOD detection that cannot be ignored. The paper does not adequately address how miscalibration—particularly severe for tail classes under imbalance—propagates through the framework. This is especially problematic since the failure of Maximum Softmax Probability (MSP) scores in OOD detection is largely attributed to poor calibration. The paper lacks analysis of how classification errors under imbalance propagate to the OOD detection framework.

**P(y) Estimation**: The estimation of P(y) is biased because the training-time data distribution (ID + auxiliary OOD) differs from the test distribution. The quantity n_o used during training cannot adequately represent the true test-time OOD distribution, making empirical prior estimates fundamentally unreliable. This raises fundamental questions about whether the learned priors πy(θ) actually solve this problem or merely provide a regularization effect.

### 3. Unjustified Learnable Prior Parameters

The transition from empirical class priors ˆπ_y to learnable parameters πy(θ) in Eq. (10) lacks principled justification. The motivation invoking "prompt tuning" is weak and the analogy does not translate convincingly to this problem. The paper argues that ˆπ_y is "sensitive to n_o" and "not precise," but provides no rigorous justification for why gradient-based learning of πy(θ) solves these issues more effectively than robust prior estimation methods from the imbalanced learning literature. Figure 2 shows that learned priors often closely resemble empirical frequencies, raising questions about the practical necessity of this additional complexity. More critically, there is no convergence analysis, no theoretical guarantee that the learned parameters represent meaningful estimates of P(y), and no explanation of what prevents these parameters from diverging during training. The ablation in Table 4 validates that learnable priors improve performance, but the mechanism remains unexplained.

### 4. Incomplete Problem Resolution

Figures A1 and A2 acknowledge that the imbalance problem is "not fully solved" even after applying ImOOD. The paper attributes this to scarce tail-class training data but does not investigate why or propose solutions beyond the current approach. This acknowledgment of incompleteness, without deeper analysis of fundamental limitations or potential remedies, weakens the overall contribution. The paper does not explore when the method fails or provide guidance on its applicability boundaries.

### 5. Modest Empirical Gains on Standard Benchmarks

The post-hoc normalization (Eq. 9) yields only marginal improvements (0.3-0.5% AUROC in Table 1), suggesting that the simple bias correction has limited practical impact. The main improvements derive from training-time regularization, which is less theoretically novel. On CIFAR benchmarks, improvements are modest (1.7% on CIFAR10-LT, 0.8-1.7% on CIFAR100-LT), and the paper provides no statistical significance testing despite these small margins. The substantially larger improvements on ImageNet-LT (5.2%) are attributed to model capacity differences (ResNet50 vs ResNet18) rather than fundamental properties of the method, raising questions about what actually drives performance differences across datasets. Additionally, the comparison with concurrent work (BalEnergy) is relegated to the appendix with different experimental settings, complicating fair assessment of relative performance.

### 6. Auxiliary OOD Data Dependency and Sensitivity

The method requires auxiliary OOD training data, a significant practical limitation that is acknowledged but not thoroughly explored. The paper does not provide sensitivity analysis regarding the choice, quantity, or distribution match of auxiliary OOD datasets. How does performance degrade with limited OOD samples or with OOD data that differs from the test distribution? The choice of auxiliary datasets (TinyImages80M for CIFAR, ImageNet-Extra for ImageNet) may favor the method, yet no ablation studies explore this critical dependency. This is particularly concerning given that the paper itself identifies that n_o cannot adequately represent test-time OOD distribution.

## Minor Weaknesses

1. **Incomplete Comparison with Related Work**: The paper identifies two failure modes but could better connect these to previous works addressing imbalanced OOD detection (e.g., [1] in the original review). Why do these prior methods fail to handle the identified cases, and what specific mechanisms in ImOOD overcome these limitations? The distinction from concurrent work like Jiang et al. (2023) remains unclear.

2. **Notation and Presentation Issues**: The notation g (the detector built upon f) should be formally defined in advance rather than introduced implicitly. Some notation inconsistencies exist (e.g., P(y) vs. πy used interchangeably). The claim that the framework is "generalized" is somewhat overstated; it represents a straightforward application of Bayes' rule.

3. **Missing Ablation Studies**: More detailed ablation studies are needed to isolate the respective contributions of P(i|x), P(y), and P(y|x,i) to the overall performance. This would help identify which components are most critical and which might be problematic.

4. **Limited Scope of Experiments**: The paper focuses primarily on long-tailed distributions with im

Summary:
The main contribution of this paper is the introduction of a generalized statistical framework called ImOOD, which addresses the problem of detecting and rejecting unknown out-of-distribution (OOD) samples in the presence of imbalanced data distributions. The paper identifies two common challenges faced by existing OOD detection methods: misclassifying tail class in-distribution (ID) samples as OOD, and incorrectly predicting OOD samples as head class ID samples. Then, the authors propose a general framework that can lead to improved detection performance.

Strengths:
The paper introduces a generalised statistical framework called ImOOD, which addresses the problem of OOD detection in the presence of imbalanced data distributions. Imbalanced data distributions widely exist in real world, and a set of fundamental works have proposed to tackle this questions to boost OOD detection. It seems that there may still exist many open questions, making the research direction in this paper promising in OOD detection.

The proposed framework, as claimed by the authors, can be used to handle imbalanced issue for a set of different scoring strategies, and the evaluation results further verify the power of their method against imbalance data more or less.

Weaknesses:
A direct question is that if we have used learning algorithms that can handle imbalanced data (which is often the case in reality) to train the basic model, do we need to handle imbalanced data for OOD detection thereafter. 

The authors identify two cases that make previous OOD detection methods fail, i.e., misclassifying tail class ID samples as OOD and incorrectly predicting OOD samples as head class ID samples. It is a direct conclusion and seemingly to be important, but could the authors further connect these two cases to previous works that handle imbalanced data in OOD detection and the proposed ImOOD. For example, why previous works, such as [1], will fail to discern these two cases and why ImOOD can overcome previous drawbacks. 

g should be defined in a proper position in advance. It seems that g should be the detector built upon f.

My main concern lies in the inaccurate estimation of beta, consisting of three terms that are all biased from my view. For P(i|X), a direct failure case is that when we have a detector whose g always greater than 0 (e.g., for distance based scoring), then P(i|X) will be always greater than 0.5. Thus, all data points will be taken as ID cases, making the estimation obviously a biased case. P(y|x,i) also suffers from biased estimation, due to the well known calibration failure of deep models. Such an issue, from my view, cannot be ignored in the field of OOD detection, since it is one of the main reason why MSP score is not effective in practice. The estimation of P(y) is also biased, since the training time data distribution (ID + OOD) is different from the test situation, thus n_o used for training cannot cover the real test situation. 


Detailed discussion about the hyper-parameters setting and the choice of auxiliary OOD data and the evaluation datasets should be discussed in detailed. More methods that handle OOD detection with imbalanced data should be considered here, such as [1]. More ablation studies to test the respective power of P(i|x), P(y), and P(y|x) should be tested.


[1] Detecting Out-of-distribution Data through In-distribution Class Prior.

Questions:
Please see the Weaknesses.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

Based on this observation, this paper investigates the widely-used MPNN and finds that MPNN is capable of capturing joint structural features theoretically and empirically. The solution is to inject orthogonality into input vectors. Equipped with the orthogonality of the input vectors, the authors propose MPLP to estimate link information and accomplish the link prediction task. The experiments show that MPLP promotes significantly in the link prediction on both attributed/non-attributed datasets.
reviews.strengths:

As I understand, the previous work believes that GNNs/MPNNs cannot do this supposing that isomorphic nodes have the same representation. So, this paper exploits the orthogonality in the input vectors, which is reasonable and does not totally contradict the previous work. This paper is basically valuable for supplementing the link prediction capability of GNNs/MPNNs in both theorems and experiments.
+ The authors propose a further discussion that some GNNs have the ability to perform link prediction tasks on attributed datasets due to the orthogonality of attributes. This is an interesting discussion that might contribute to the incense reason why GNNs/MPNNs can perform link prediction on attributed benchmarks. The empirical findings in Table 4 bring about the phenomenon and leave the issues that are worth discussing.

Summary:
Given that the representations of isomorphic nodes are the same, their link that aggregates the representations cannot capture some structural representation like CN, and thus it is intractable to perform the link prediction task. Based on this observation, this paper investigates the widely-used MPNN and finds that MPNN is capable of capturing joint structural features theoretically and empirically. The solution is to inject orthogonality into input vectors. Equipped with the orthogonality of the input vectors, the authors propose MPLP to estimate link information and accomplish the link prediction task. The experiments show that MPLP promotes significantly in the link prediction on both attributed/non-attributed datasets.

Strengths:
+ This paper is a meaningful discussion of the previous work (Zhang et al., 2021) on whether GNNs/MPNNs can capture structural link representation. As I understand, the previous work believes that GNNs/MPNNs cannot do this supposing that isomorphic nodes have the same representation. So, this paper exploits the orthogonality in the input vectors, which is reasonable and does not totally contradict the previous work. This paper is basically valuable for supplementing the link prediction capability of GNNs/MPNNs in both theorems and experiments.
+ The authors propose a further discussion that some GNNs have the ability to perform link prediction tasks on attributed datasets due to the orthogonality of attributes. This is an interesting discussion that might contribute to the incense reason why GNNs/MPNNs can perform link prediction on attributed benchmarks. The empirical findings in Table 4 bring about the phenomenon and leave the issues that are worth discussing.
+ The results demonstrate that MPLP outperforms the state-of-the-art baselines on both attributed/non-attributed benchmarks.

Weaknesses:
- The assumption of Gaussian distribution in the initialization of input vectors and weight matrices is reasonable for non-attributed benchmarks but it seems not to be correspondent for attributed benchmarks.
- The research focuses on the capability of MPNN for estimating common neighbors which is a critical heuristic for link prediction. In other words, MPLP injects the CN heuristic into MPNN. So, what about the link prediction performance when we directly regard CN as the node attribute? For example, the first elements of the input vectors could be substituted with CN value.

Questions:
- Why are the results of SAGE on some benchmarks (Computers/Photo) different between Table 2 and Table 4?
- I am curious about when we concatenate the attribute vector with random feats, would the results be better than those with only attributes or only random feats? Since it is hard to evaluate whether attribute vectors are more important than random feats before conducting the experiments, the concatenation might achieve the better one automatically.
- What does the author mean by `MPLP holds its own’? What proves that?
- What is the potential impact when you find MPNN can promote link prediction? In what cases or scenarios will MPLP be used and benefit the industry?

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
# Hybrid Peer Review

## Summary

This paper investigates a fundamental question about Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs): whether they can capture structural link representations necessary for link prediction, despite the constraint that isomorphic nodes receive identical representations. The authors provide theoretical analysis (Theorems 1-3) demonstrating that MPNNs equipped with quasi-orthogonal (QO) input vectors can approximate common neighbor (CN) and related heuristic link predictors. Building on these insights, they propose MPLP (Message Passing Link Predictor), which combines QO vector propagation with structural feature estimation via distance encoding and triangle counting. Experiments across 13 benchmarks demonstrate that MPLP outperforms state-of-the-art baselines including SEAL, ELPH, and NCNC on 12 of 13 datasets.

## Strengths

+ **Meaningful theoretical contribution**: This paper provides a valuable response to prior work (Zhang et al., 2021) questioning whether GNNs/MPNNs can capture structural link representations. The authors' insight—that orthogonal input vectors enable MPNNs to overcome the isomorphic node representation limitation—is reasonable and does not contradict prior work but rather supplements it constructively. Theorems 1-3 offer formal analysis connecting quasi-orthogonality to CN estimation, providing rigorous insights into GNN expressiveness for link prediction.

+ **Interesting discussion on attributed datasets**: The paper contributes a valuable observation that some GNNs perform link prediction on attributed datasets due to inherent orthogonality of node attributes themselves. This insight helps explain empirical phenomena observed in prior work. The empirical findings in Table 4 and Appendix C documenting when random vectors match or exceed real attributes deserve attention and raise important questions about the role of different information sources in link prediction.

+ **Comprehensive experimental validation**: The experimental evaluation is thorough, covering 8 non-attributed and 5 attributed benchmarks with multiple baselines, detailed ablation studies (Tables 5-12), and analysis of different metrics (Hits@20, @50, @100). The paper includes extensive appendices with hyperparameter sensitivity analysis, variance characterization, and complete proofs, demonstrating reproducibility and transparency.

+ **Strong empirical results**: MPLP achieves competitive or superior performance on 12 of 13 benchmarks with improvements ranging from 2-10% over closest competitors. The method also demonstrates practical efficiency with competitive inference time and memory usage compared to much larger models like SEAL and ELPH.

+ **Practical efficiency**: The stripped-down variant MPLP(no feat) achieves strong performance with only 260 parameters, demonstrating that the structural features captured by QO vectors are the primary drivers of performance. This efficiency is noteworthy compared to baselines requiring substantially more parameters.

## Weaknesses

- **Limited technical novelty**: The core technical contributions heavily build upon DotHash (Nunes et al., 2023). The use of quasi-orthogonal vectors, hypercube sampling, and the basic message-passing framework are largely borrowed from prior work. The main additions—one-hot hub encoding and norm rescaling—represent incremental engineering choices rather than fundamental methodological innovations. Beyond QO vectors, MPLP integrates existing techniques including distance encoding (Li et al., 2020), shortcut removal (SEAL/ELPH), and triangle estimation (Chen et al., 2020). While integration of these components is competent, the contribution is more engineering-focused than conceptually novel.

- **Theory-practice gap regarding initialization assumptions**: Theorems 1-3 critically depend on random vector initialization from Gaussian or hypercube distributions. However, in practice, when node attributes exist (attributed datasets), the theoretical assumptions are violated. The paper states this assumption "seems not to be correspondent for attributed benchmarks" but does not adequately bridge this gap. Furthermore, while the theory assumes random weight matrix initialization, standard GNN training involves learning these weights, which may violate the theoretical guarantees. The connection between random initialization assumptions and the learned representations actually used during inference requires more rigorous treatment.

- **Generalization and deployment concerns**: The reliance on random QO vectors as node signatures raises fundamental questions about generalization. How does MPLP handle new nodes added after training? Since random vectors are node-specific, new nodes require new random vectors. Does this maintain the theoretical guarantees of Theorem 2? The paper briefly mentions limited generalization to unseen nodes (Section 3.2) but does not thoroughly investigate inductive settings or dynamic graph scenarios, which are practically important.

- **Training-inference cost asymmetry**: The paper acknowledges in Appendix F that training cost "significantly outweighs" inference cost due to shortcut removal requiring recomputation of shortest-path neighborhoods for each batch, resulting in O(tdr + |V|dr + tdrF) training complexity versus O(tF) inference complexity. This is a substantial practical limitation that deserves more discussion in the main paper. While masking is mentioned as a potential solution in Appendix F, this alternative is not thoroughly investigated. The actual wall-clock training time compared to SEAL and ELPH deserves explicit reporting.

- **Incomplete variance reduction**: While Theorem 2 characterizes variance as depending on degree products (du·dv), the one-hot hub strategy is motivated empirically but lacks theoretical justification. More critically, the paper acknowledges (Appendix D.1) that this approach loses effectiveness for multi-hop features (Figure 8), suggesting the solution is incomplete and may not scale to deeper structural features. The fundamental variance issue in dense graphs remains inadequately resolved.

- **Inconsistent improvements and statistical rigor**: While MPLP wins on 12 of 13 datasets, improvements are often modest (2-5%), with standard deviations overlapping in many cases. On some datasets (e.g., Power: 32.25 vs. SEAL: 61.47), MPLP significantly underperforms, yet the paper provides limited analysis of these failure cases. Statistical significance testing is absent—have differences between MPLP and runner-up methods been validated through t-tests or similar procedures? Without significance testing, it is unclear whether observed improvements are meaningful.

- **Unclear role of node attributes**: Appendix C reveals that random vectors sometimes match or exceed performance of real node attributes (Computers, Photo datasets). This contradicts the stated motivation of "preserving node-level complexities" and raises fundamental questions: Does MPLP truly leverage attribute information, or does it primarily exploit QO structure? How much of the performance comes from structural features versus semantic information in attributes? This deserves investigation in the main text rather than being relegated to appendices.

- **Hyperparameter sensitivity**: Tables 9-10 demonstrate dramatic performance drops at certain batch sizes and other hyperparameter settings, suggesting the method is sensitive to tuning choices. The paper does not provide principled guidance for practitioners on setting batch size B, hub threshold b, or other hyperparameters for new datasets. This limits practical applicability.

## Questions and Concerns

1. **Theory-practice alignment**: How well do the theoretical assumptions regarding quasi-orthogonality and random initialization hold when the model uses learned GNN representations? Can you provide empirical validation of actual orthogonality in learned features, not just theoretical guarantees?

2. **Generalization to unseen nodes**: Beyond the brief mention in Section 3.2, how does MPLP perform in inductive settings where test nodes are not seen during training? How sensitive is performance to the random seed chosen for new node signatures?

3. **Variance reduction optimality**: Why does the one-hot hub strategy lose effectiveness for 2-hop features? Have you explored alternative variance reduction techniques (e.g., importance sampling, control variates)? Is there a principled way to determine when variance reduction becomes ineffective?

4. **Comparison with simpler baselines**: Have you compared MPLP against simpler alternatives, such as using CN/AA/RA with learned weights, or directly incorporating CN as a node attribute (as suggested in the main review)? This would better isolate the contribution of message-passing versus structural heuristics.

5. **Training cost justification**: What is the actual wall-clock training time compared to SEAL, ELPH, and NCNC on large graphs? Is the performance gain worth the significant training overhead? Have you thoroughly investigated masking as an alternative to actual edge removal?

6. **Failure case analysis**: Why does MPLP significantly underperform on the Power dataset (32.25 vs. 61.47 for SEAL) and show marginal improvements on Router? What graph properties or structural characteristics cause these failures? Understanding these cases would strengthen the paper.

7. **Attribute importance**: In light of Appendix C findings, can you provide clearer guidance on when node attributes are actually beneficial versus when QO structure dominates? This has important implications for practitioners deciding whether to use MPLP on attributed versus non-attributed graphs.

8. **Differences in reported results**: Why do the results for SAGE differ between Table 2 and Table 4 on some benchmarks (Computers/Photo)

reviews.summary: In this paper, the authors explore the use of pure Message-Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) for link prediction in graphs. The paper starts by exploring the known limitations of MPNNs related to their permutation invariance property for link prediction and introduces an approach called Message Passing Link Predictor (MPLP) that leverages a node "signature". This feature vector essentially consists of a one-hot quasi-orthogonal vector, which the authors claim as a solution for MPNNs and more accurate link prediction. Experimental validation on 13 different graph datasets, both attributed and non-attributed is performed in order to concretely validate the claims made in the paper.
reviews.strengths:
- Originality: Pure Message-Passing for Link Prediction is not as well-understood as it should be, so the idea of enhancing GNNs to better handle the estimation of heuristic methods is a strong avenue for generating original work. MPLP does a fine job of this through it's problem formulation, equations, and theorems. It also takes care of tying in their motivations to numerous other relevant papers. 
- Quality: The spread of datasets captures a variety of domains and the experiments are structured in a ways that directly supports the author's claims.
- Clarity: The principles for the theorems and experiments are well-founded, it respects the reader's background in understanding related GNN works and keeps the discussion about any innovation presented in the paper succinct.
- Significance: The use of quasi-orthogonal vectors as a means of enhancing the message-passing capabilities of GNNs is entirely novel as far as I know, albeit it does extend principles from SOTA models. Future research could be inspired by a high-level approach that is similar to MPLP. MPLP's ability to estimate CN and DE is also a promising and significant improvement for GNN's ability to conduct link prediction. The non-attributed benchmark test, as shown in Table 1, is an interesting inclusion that speaks to the power of MPLP to handle non-standard link prediction scenarios. The inference experiments are thorough, considering the estimation of multple labels in regard to signatue dimension, inference, and ablation studies on batch-size.
reviews.weaknesses:
- The link prediction results for attributed benchmarks, as shown in Table 2 is limited in that it does not include results from all of the standard datasets: ogbl-ppa, ogbl-ddi, ogbl-citation2. The OGBL datasets are included as baselines in all of the included SOTA models, the results from which would serve as a direct comparison for MPLP's performance versus any SOTA method. 
- The first concern is compounded in that it is difficult to truly tell how well MPLP improves estimation of labels given that it only considers ogbl-collab versus ELPH and not the more scalable BUDDY. 
- The experiments, as shown in Table 1 and 2 only consider results for Hits@50. This seems relevant for the subsequent evaluations of inference, dimensions, and batch sizes. But, is still limited since the current SOTA models run experiments with Hits@100, Hits@20, and MRR.
- The test for the estimation capabilities seems limited by testing just GCN and SAGE. The inner product calculation seems like it should be extended to other types of popular GNNs such as: GIN and GAT. The results from which would lend more credence to the claims made about expressiveness and the effects of implementing quasi-orthogonal vectors into link prediction models. 
- The node-label estimation of CN and DE is promising. However, it seems limited in scope since it approximates just CN or DE and does not extend further to DRNL or DE+. This may be due to concerns of tractable computation but given SEAL's explicit testing of both DRNL and DE++ as labelling tricks, this seems like an important inclusion to evaluate MPLP fully.
reviews.questions:
- What was the reasoning behind not including the remaining OGBL datasets? It seems that MPLP's scalability is an important component based on the inference, dimensions, and ablation studies but why not bring the scalability to the forefront of the paper instead of placing the ablation studies in the appendix.
- The extent that GAT could be effectively tested against MPLP seems limited given that GAT relies on attention and MPLP does not have explicitly have a mechanism to consider this. However, when considering the expressiveness of MPLP with random features and it's ability to estimate triangles, why not include a provably expressive GNN to test like GIN?
- What sort of limitations would integrating DRNL into GCN or SAGE pose? Is it concern over tractability and the fact that in certain instances DRNL is similar to DE and CN?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this paper, the authors explore the use of pure Message-Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) for link prediction in graphs. The paper starts by exploring the known limitations of MPNNs related to their permutation invariance property for link prediction and introduces an approach called Message Passing Link Predictor (MPLP) that leverages a node "signature". This feature vector essentially consists of a one-hot quasi-orthogonal vector, which the authors claim as a solution for MPNNs and more accurate link prediction. Experimental validation on 13 different graph datasets, both attributed and non-attributed is performed in order to concretely validate the claims made in the paper.

Strengths:
- Originality: Pure Message-Passing for Link Prediction is not as well-understood as it should be, so the idea of enhancing GNNs to better handle the estimation of heuristic methods is a strong avenue for generating original work. MPLP does a fine job of this through it's problem formulation, equations, and theorems. It also takes care of tying in their motivations to numerous other relevant papers. 
- Quality: The spread of datasets captures a variety of domains and the experiments are structured in a ways that directly supports the author's claims.
- Clarity: The principles for the theorems and experiments are well-founded, it respects the reader's background in understanding related GNN works and keeps the discussion about any innovation presented in the paper succinct.
- Significance: The use of quasi-orthogonal vectors as a means of enhancing the message-passing capabilities of GNNs is entirely novel as far as I know, albeit it does extend principles from SOTA models. Future research could be inspired by a high-level approach that is similar to MPLP. MPLP's ability to estimate CN and DE is also a promising and significant improvement for GNN's ability to conduct link prediction. The non-attributed benchmark test, as shown in Table 1, is an interesting inclusion that speaks to the power of MPLP to handle non-standard link prediction scenarios. The inference experiments are thorough, considering the estimation of multple labels in regard to signatue dimension, inference, and ablation studies on batch-size.

Weaknesses:
- The link prediction results for attributed benchmarks, as shown in Table 2 is limited in that it does not include results from all of the standard datasets: ogbl-ppa, ogbl-ddi, ogbl-citation2. The OGBL datasets are included as baselines in all of the included SOTA models, the results from which would serve as a direct comparison for MPLP's performance versus any SOTA method. 
- The first concern is compounded in that it is difficult to truly tell how well MPLP improves estimation of labels given that it only considers ogbl-collab versus ELPH and not the more scalable BUDDY. 
- The experiments, as shown in Table 1 and 2 only consider results for Hits@50. This seems relevant for the subsequent evaluations of inference, dimensions, and batch sizes. But, is still limited since the current SOTA models run experiments with Hits@100, Hits@20, and MRR.
- The test for the estimation capabilities seems limited by testing just GCN and SAGE. The inner product calculation seems like it should be extended to other types of popular GNNs such as: GIN and GAT. The results from which would lend more credence to the claims made about expressiveness and the effects of implementing quasi-orthogonal vectors into link prediction models. 
- The node-label estimation of CN and DE is promising. However, it seems limited in scope since it approximates just CN or DE and does not extend further to DRNL or DE+. This may be due to concerns of tractable computation but given SEAL's explicit testing of both DRNL and DE++ as labelling tricks, this seems like an important inclusion to evaluate MPLP fully.

Questions:
- What was the reasoning behind not including the remaining OGBL datasets? It seems that MPLP's scalability is an important component based on the inference, dimensions, and ablation studies but why not bring the scalability to the forefront of the paper instead of placing the ablation studies in the appendix.
- The extent that GAT could be effectively tested against MPLP seems limited given that GAT relies on attention and MPLP does not have explicitly have a mechanism to consider this. However, when considering the expressiveness of MPLP with random features and it's ability to estimate triangles, why not include a provably expressive GNN to test like GIN?
- What sort of limitations would integrating DRNL into GCN or SAGE pose? Is it concern over tractability and the fact that in certain instances DRNL is similar to DE and CN?
